period-1/abbasid-caliphate.html.title: Abbasid Caliphate
period-1/abbasid-caliphate.html.description: The major ARAB caliphate, during its reign was the Golden Age of Islam. Slowly, it did lose power in the Middle east but the final blow was only when the Mongols arrived and sacked Baghdad, turning it into the il-Khanate. It supported many centers of cultural learning such as Baghdad and the House of Wisdom, and the growth of trade was supported by these cultural and intellectual hubs that attracted merchants. After their fall, they would be replaced by the Turks, but their influence in creating such strong cultural sites still remained, especially in the technologies that H.O.W scholars developed.

period-1/al-andalus.html.title: Al-Andalus
period-1/al-andalus.html.description: Al-Andalus, by itself wasn't a huge power, but was the essential transfer point for how the ideas of Dar-al Islam transferred onto W. Europe, especially during the Crusades. Serving as this connection point, al-Andalus helped bring out ideas into the wider world and disperse goods & technology into Western Europe, helping create the basis for the Renaissance.

period-1/aztecs.html.title: Aztecs
period-1/aztecs.html.description:
The Aztecs were the most powerful state in Central America during this time period, and were notable for their heavy use of their military. They were extremely militarized, with their justification and purpose being blood sacrifice funded by Flowery Wars. They would bring other nations under their empire through conquest, and they would claim tribute from conquered nations.
While they didn't have as much cross city-state trade interaction, their capital Tenochtitlan was extremely modern, and they had already began commercialization and urbanization in Tenochtitlan.

period-1/baghdad.html.title: Baghdad
period-1/baghdad.html.description:
An important city-state and the heart of the Abbasid Caliphate, its sacking led to the death of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Similar to other major city states such as Timbuktu or Tenochitlan, Baghdad was vitally important as a cultural and trade hub in the Abbasid Caliphate and contributed much to its dominance - due to the House of Wisdom, it attracted scholars from different cultures and regions that greatly contributed to technological development and the prevalence of Dar al-Islam, examples include Avicenna and the Canon of Medicine or Nasir al-Din al-Tusi and geometry.

period-1/bananas-(and-other-trade-goods).title: Bananas (and other trade goods)
period-1/bananas-(and-other-trade-goods).description:
Bananas were a new good from the land of the Zanj (Swahili Trade States) and were a new food that is evidence of widespread trade and interconnected global networks. They would eventually spread to mostly be grown in Southeast Asia and eventually, the Americas (but not yet).
Champa Rice was a valuable trade good that was gained in tribute and given to China, and is an example of how interconnected networks of trade led to increased economic production, and how important trade was to gain access to new goods.
Luxury goods (ivory, silk, porcelain) were also traded during this time period, and demand would increase dramatically. While luxury goods were a cause for new trade routes due to their high demand, the effect was that the producers of luxury goods would become extremely wealthy, like the gold nation of the Mali Empire or China.

period-1/blood-sacrifice.html.title: Blood Sacrifice
period-1/blood-sacrifice.html.description:
Blood sacrifices were made in order to stave off the Aztec version of Ragnarök, where they believed that without blood sacrifices, they would all die.
Blood sacrifice legitimized the power of Aztec kings, by instilling fear into the population and to their enemies. Blood sacrifice was also intertwined with religion, also having a positive way to legitimize their power - Aztec kings/priests (higher classes) were doing good by not ending the world. Thus, not only were they using the threat of blood sacrifice to legitimize their rule, but also using their religion as a way to justify their power.

period-1/bubonic-plague.html.title: Bubonic Plague
period-1/bubonic-plague.html.description:
The bubonic plague is a NEGATIVE effect that occured due to growing trade networks, and it was started at the Siege of Caffa by the Mongols applying biological warfare. It would kill and decimate Europe and the rest of the world due to poor sanitation, and would cause so much harm that 1/3 of the world population would die off. It was extremely destructive, and had global reach.
Due to growing trade networks, the bubonic plague was able to spread much more rapidly and much further than previous epidemics. Without the growth of trade networks, and the movement of merchants, the bubonic plague would not have been able to spread as far.

period-1/caravanserai.html.title: Caravanserai
period-1/caravanserai.html.description:
Secure rest stops and inns that helped secure the trade routes, making long-distance trading much easier. Similar to the Yam System, see for more details on why improved security helped
Another effect that occurred with the rise of caravanserai was that traders not only could rest and trade but they could also connect with one another, helping increase cross-cultural connections as merchants met in caravanserai (see people like Ibn Battuta)

period-1/catholic-church.html.title: Catholic Church
period-1/catholic-church.html.description: The Catholic Church/Pope crowned kings/lords, and lords in feudalism needed to justify their rule by getting the permission of priests/pope (god, Divine Right of Kings). The Catholic Church and the feudal system justified their rule by using religion, due to heavy encouragement of Christianity in Europe - thus, with the support of the church, peasants would be less likely to rebel. The Crusades are an example of the Catholic Church legitimizing their rule, by attempting to adopt a religious mission and attempt to gain power and territory.

period-1/censorate.html.title: Censorate
period-1/censorate.html.description: An investigative force of the chinese empire, it was mainly a conservative force, serving to put fear into bureaucrats and it would prevent radical reforms, persisting until the overthrow of the Qing dynasty. It would help investigate corruption in many of the ministries, but was mainly used to keep the Scholar-officials in check and in fear, helping China maintain power.

period-1/champa-rice.html.title: Champa Rice
period-1/champa-rice.html.description:
Champa rice was a strain of rice that allowed China's population to increase heavily, and it was gained by the tributary system in one of Vietnam's tributes.
Champa rice as a good has no other comparison, as it was a critical bulk food source. While other important crops are like “cash crops”, such as spices, the closest thing you can get to Champa rice, a good that actually directly improves living standards is Bananas (and other trade goods), yet Champa rice drove China's population so much more than bananas did for their respective states.
This helped increase China's productivity during this time, and increased economic wealth helped lead to a stable and happy population, legitimizing their rule, especially important to maintain the Mandate Of Heaven

period-1/china.html.title: China
period-1/china.html.description:
China is important as the most complete and strongest nation during this time, and will be so until P3 (there's a lot of evidence that China, due to the silver market was the key producer of luxury goods)
China is the biggest producer and exporter of luxury goods such as silk, porcelain, and tea, all highly valuable goods that were traded along the Silk Road - trade was driven by China and the demand for China's luxury goods. As trade expanded, so did demand for Chinese goods, especially in Europe.
China, as a benefit of being highly advanced, also developed many leading technologies at the time that were also dispersed

period-1/commercialization.html.title: Commercialization
period-1/commercialization.html.description:
Commericialization is the change from a hand-to-mouth economy to one where prodiucers and farmers can produce excess and sell it for profit, turning teir job into one that mainly focuses on selling excess goods. This development occurred throughout most states during this time period, and was caused by increased prosperity due to spreading technologies which improved living conditions.
However, this effect also occurred independently of trade, and commercialization would also become a cause of increased trade as producers sought to sell their goods to others. In general, any increase in living conditions would help lead to commercialization, which almost always improved the economy of a state and led to the rise of a middle class.

period-1/confucianism.html.title: Confucianism
period-1/confucianism.html.description:
A belief system that heavily influenced chinese thinking, this is HUGE and contributes to the overall trajectory of China - HUGELY IMPORTANT!
Respecting one's elders: a fundamental part of Chinese and Confucianist society. Filial piety helped reinforce the 5 Relationships and the clear social structure of Chinese society, making it even more difficult to rebel and leave one's social class as it acts as a cultural tie to maintain social hierarchies.
The 5 key relationships: husband-wife, peer-peer, ruler-subject, father-son, elder-younger sibling, were core relationships and had clear superior inferior interactions in Confucianism. This helped create a social hierarchy, making it easier for a ruler to govern. Another effect is in gender roles, where it developed and created a patriarchal society which can be seen through acts such as Foot Binding.Meritocratic examinations that anyone could take, leading to the creation of a scholar-official bureaucratic class. However, it was still unbalanced and unfair due to tutoring, much like the SAT and other "standardized" exams today, rich families could afford the tutors necessary, but sometimes poor towns would sponsor someone. By allowing limited social ability, rulers gave commoners a way to improve their status keeping commoners more accepting of their lowered social status (they "deserved" it). However, the prohibitive cost also served to still keep commoners out of higher social levels, and helped keep the rulers in power.
The most important thing was that in BOTH the Ming and Song dynasties, Confucianism was a continuity. This continuity is important because it shows us how even in the face of the Mongols, China itself changed little to accommodate for them, culturally and socially, and the major changes were economic and political. The "heart" of China stayed the same.

period-1/development-of-trade-states.htm.title: Development of Trade States
period-1/development-of-trade-states.htm.description: Another key development during this time period was the creation of trade-based states, new nations appeared that utilized only trade to grow their wealth and power. These states demonstrate the impact of trade in P1, as nations grew to rely on trade. States such as the Mali Empire, Malaccan Sultanate, and the Swahili Trade States would all rely on trade as the basis for their economy. This led to a cyclical effect where as trade based states grew in power, they increased their trade output and thus grew the very networks they relied on.

period-1/diasporic-merchant-communities..title: Diasporic Merchant Communities
period-1/diasporic-merchant-communities..description: Trade, due to monsoon winds, would lead to diasporic merchant communities, as evidenced by intermarriage of merchants and communities in states like Kilwa/Swahili states that use Arabic + Bantu as their language. This intermixing of different cultures lead to Religious Syncretism and the spread of religion especially Islam (Dar al-Islam).

period-1/divine-right-of-kings.html.title: Divine Right of Kings
period-1/divine-right-of-kings.html.description: The DRoK was the justification of Kings during this time period, where they justified their rule by using God and Christianity as the justification, believing that God meant for this King to rule. DRoK is an example of religion being used to control and maintain power by states, such as the Mandate Of Heaven, Blood Sacrifice, and Islam (Mansa Musa). This led to a cyclical relationship with the Church, while Kings had more political power technically the Pope (who could excommunicate) was more powerful as they could attack their justification. This becomes important later on in Period 2, with new branches of Christianity, as kingdoms adopt new branches to protect their justifications.

period-1/europe.html.title: Europe
period-1/europe.html.description: There's not much to say, as Europe was far, far behind all other states during this time. They would start catching up in P2 and eventually be ahead in P3/4, but for now they're a series of disconnected feudal states with no centralized rule. Italian trade city-states do exist, (Venice, Genoa) however, and are a great example of Development of Trade States in different regions. Europe did participate in world trade, and the silk road would lead to events in P2 - extreme demand for luxury goods and the desire to bypass middlemen

period-1/feudalism.html.title: Feudalism
period-1/feudalism.html.description: Feudalism is the social and political structure of Europe, it is decentralized with knights swearing loyalty to land owning nobles, and serfs swearing loyalty to knights/lords as well. Note that it's decentralized, there are no large nation states yet. The social structure of feudalism clearly establishes superiors and inferiors, like Confucianism and forces nations/states to legitimize their power through the church or the support of the nobility. This leads to situations such as potential excommunication to delegitimize rulers as the sole cause of rule that could be justified was Divine Right of Kings. Feudalism caused the political structure of Europe to be politically disconnected and fragmented, as there was no powerful central ruler.

period-1/flowery-wars.html.title: Flowery Wars
period-1/flowery-wars.html.description: Wars specifically to get bodies for Blood Sacrifice, which instilled fear into other states, not just internally. This led to less rebellions from their tributary states. These religious wars were a way for the Aztecs to justify spreading fear and expanding territory, especially gaining new tributary states. These wars of domination also helped legitimize their empire, by supporting their religious foundation of blood sacrifice and instilling fear into other states/internally to maintain control.

period-1/gender-parallelism.html.title: Gender Parallelism
period-1/gender-parallelism.html.description: A social system where men and women aren’t necessarily better or worse than one another, they just work in separate fields. They aren’t superior or inferior but work different jobs, thus the “parallelism”: By allowing different genders to still work and have power, rulers can help mitigate rebellions and unrest due to inequality. Furthermore, gender parallelism makes work more efficient and is a way to form a division of labor, making a state more productive and thus legitimizing a state that can provide more for its people. Both the Aztecs and the Mongols had examples of gender parallelism, in the Mongols, women played a vital logistic role of setting up camps and travelling with men to war while men were the physical soldiers. The most significant part is that both genders, while working different roles and may still have their "spheres", have equal rights and are treated equally regardless.

period-1/golden-horde.html.title: Golden Horde
period-1/golden-horde.html.description:
The Khanate of the Golden Horde was the name that the Russians gave to the Mongols, and controlled Russia. This Khanate allowed princes to be autonomous, and there was little impact politically and culturally - there was the least assimilation on the Mongol's part here. However, by letting princes be autonomous, they weakened their own control and made it easier to rebel as princes gained centralized power and gained wealth.
Another effect of their actions by staying hands-off was the survival of the Eastern Orthodox Church, which they did NOT destroy.

period-1/grand-canal.html.title: Grand Canal
period-1/grand-canal.html.description: While the Grand Canal itself was built and predated the Song dynasty, during the Song dynasty they extended the Canal and made a lot of general improvements to the grand canal, helping control floods and also increase agricultural productivity. This helps China improve and helps them maintain their rule as this economic improvement helps stability. Combined with a highly commercialized economy, the increased Grand Canal allowed for trade and huge economic output, leading to the so-called Song Economic Revolution. All of this helped legitimize their government and helped them keep the Mandate Of Heaven

period-1/house-of-wisdom.html.title: House of Wisdom
period-1/house-of-wisdom.html.description:
An institution that was created in the Abbasid Caliphate that greatly contributed to Dar al-Islam's dominance in technological development in the sciences.
By preserving Greco-Roman knowledge, they also contributed heavily to the Renaissance, and helped preserve ancient Greco-Roman innovations along with developing new technological innovations.
The House of Wisdom helped strengthen the Abbasid Caliphate by maintaining strong technological and cultural flourishing, and the successes and artistic flourishing of the Golden Age of Islam legitimized the Abbasid Caliphate even after political and military defeats.

period-1/hueztilopothcli.html.title: Hueztilopothcli
period-1/hueztilopothcli.html.description:
The sun god of the Aztecs, with all blood sacrifices being made to him. The Aztecs believed that without giving him life energy through blood, Armageddon would occur.
The Aztecs would use Hueztiloptochopqwpeipli as a religious justification and by enforcing a state religion, making it easier for them to govern the general population - without the promotion of this religion by the state as a whole, they could not justify their large military, slavery, and expanstion to the general population.
This also gives them a sacred mission to legitimize the Aztec's rule.

period-1/ibn-battuta.html.title: Ibn Battuta
period-1/ibn-battuta.html.description:
A traveler who wrote accounts and travel literature, he extensively traveled across the IOB and his accounts were valuable to provide insight into life there. Other explorers and traders also sold travel literature in this time period, and increased knowledge of city states helped traders and travelers travel across dar Al-Islam, travel literature like the Rihala was useful to merchants and gave critical information they might need to travel.
One of the effects and the causes of increased trade were new travelers, who were able to travel across the world and also act as merchants. These travelers disseminated travel information, but also used information from increased trade.
Thus, these new travelers led to increased cross-regional interaction and also disseminated information about these new interactions.

period-1/il-khanate.html.title: il-Khanate
period-1/il-khanate.html.description: The Persian Khanate of the Mongols, after the sacking of Baghdad, they destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate and ended the Arab rule of Islam (leading to the rise of the Turks). The Mongols would assimilate into Muslim/Persian culture and adapt their customs to maintain their rule. The formation of the il-Khanate was significant and changed politics in the Middle East due to their ending of the Abassid caliphate and the ensuing power vacuum leading to the rise of the Turks in Islam, such as the Mamluks.

period-1/incan.html.title: Incan
period-1/incan.html.description: Essentially ancient socialists, the Incan empire was characterized by being peaceful and sharing resources communally, but still invading other territories. They would invite states to become tributary states and join their empire and would conquer them if they declined. They were extremely skilled at administration over their entire empire and created many great public works, increasing trade networks in south america.

period-1/india.html.title: India
period-1/india.html.description: India at this time period was still not complete and a set of separate states, some of which did control large amounts of territory, but the entire state didn't exist yet. The strongest out of these was probably the Dehli sultanate, important because it's ANOTHER example of the success of Sufis and the spread of Islam. India is also important because it's also technologically advanced and is a key exporter, similar to China - they developed many things such as the 0 number system, important for foundational mathematics...

period-1/islam.html.title: Islam
period-1/islam.html.description: Islam is one of the main Abrahamic religions, and is relatively recent. It has a lot of traits and terminology... as a basic rundown, Muslims believe in parts of other Abrahamic religions (Judaism and Christianity) but ultimately believe that these have diverged and hold their own holy scripture, the Quran, as truth. They follow the Sharia law, religious law that governs all aspects of law. This basic aspect of Muslim nations is part of what makes Islam appealing to adopt by leaders due to the strict laws it already has built into it. Another important aspect of Muslims are the Sufis, religious missionaries, who are extremely important during this time to spread Muslim thought and Islam.

period-1/malaccan-sultanate.html.title: Malaccan Sultanate
period-1/malaccan-sultanate.html.description:
The strait of Malacca was a hugely important strategic strait that controlled the S.E asia trade route with China and India. Notably, the Malaccan Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate, demonstrating the reach of Islam during this time. They also demonstrate the extent of Sufis, Muslim missionaries, who brought Islam to the Spice Islands.
Similar to the Swahili Trade States, the Malaccan Sultanate was trade-based state, focusing on taxing trade through the strait in order to generate profit to fund its economy and military. The Malaccan Sultanate demonstrates that trade could become the basis of a nation as networks of exchange developed during this time period.

period-1/mali-empire.html.title: Mali Empire
period-1/mali-empire.html.description: The gold empire of West Africa in this time period, replaced by  the Songhai empire BUT because of Mansa Musa’s efforts, their Muslim ideas were continued to the next empires, and thus Africa adopted Islam.The Mali empire, similar to the idea of the Pax Mongolica, created a similar Pax Mandinka, where the Mali empire sponsored trade and allowed trade to flourish. Similar to the Yam System, the Mali empire deterred bandits and secured trade routes.

period-1/mamluk-sultanate.html.title: Mamluk Sultanate
period-1/mamluk-sultanate.html.description: The replacement of the il-Khanate, VERY IMPORTANT as an example of a key development: Dar al-Islam turned from Arabs to Turks, and the Mamluks are a clear example of this. They were similar to the Mongols in strategy and originally started as slaves.

period-1/mandate-of-heaven.html.title: Mandate Of Heaven
period-1/mandate-of-heaven.html.description:
The parallel of Divine Right of Kings in China, the mandate of heaven essentially stated that dynasties had the right to rule and also the right to be overthrown as soon as they became tyrannic. Thus, most dynasties would attempt to use legitimacy and not direct control in order to maintain power over their population, knowing that direct control would lead to a rebellion.
However, this also helped them, as with economic growth and a commercialized economy the Song would be able to legitimize themselves for centuries due to their economic revolution.

period-1/manorialism.html.title: Manorialism
period-1/manorialism.html.description:
The economic structure of Europe goes hand in hand with feudalism. Each plot of land, a fief, is also a “manor” and each one is self-sufficient.
By being self-sufficient, each state didn’t have to depend on trade and thus could survive independently. However, it also stopped Europe's economic and trade development, due to the fact that each manor was self-sufficient, limiting Europe's participation in the new global trade routes.
By keeping each fief self-sufficient, lords and nobles could divide their rule into fiefs, making it easier to administrate, as a noble only had to control some lords and each lords some knights etc. etc.

period-1/mansa-musa.html.title: Mansa Musa
period-1/mansa-musa.html.description: The richest man during this time, mainly remembered for his expensive Hajj, where he made his pilgrimage to Mecca. It was ridiculously expensive, where Mansa Musa threw money and gold everywhere, additionally building Mosques, contributing to the spread of Islam in Africa. Mansa Musa was a devout Muslim, and after completing his Hajj, he was even more devout and essentially converted the entirety of the Mali empire into a Muslim state. This Hajj helped legitimize the Mali Empire by demonstrating the immense wealth of the Mali empire. As a secondary goal, it also led to the spread of Islam in Africa as well, cementing African empires in the use of Islam to maintain power. Another effect of his hajj was the legends it created, leading Europeans to flock to Africa to try and find wealth (the Catalan Atlas and that one image)

period-1/mi'ta-system.html.title: Mi'ta System
period-1/mi'ta-system.html.description: A system of forced labor, where the Incans would take a labor tax in order to fund large building projects such as mountain roads/irrigation. This labor tax (corvee) would become a continuity in the Spanish empire, and helped the Incan empire maintain their power as it helped construct many large public projects, further developing their empire and also ensuring that their states remained loyal and governed under the central government

period-1/mountain-roads.html.title: Mountain Roads
period-1/mountain-roads.html.description:
The Incas created a huge mountain road system to increase the ease of communication across their vast empire, constructing one of the largest communication systems in one of the harshest environments to build roads in (interaction with environment).
The Incan empire utilized this road system to great effect for military and also commercial use, and it played a crucial role in helping maintain central control over their empire.

period-1/muslim-berbers.html.title: Muslim Berbers
period-1/muslim-berbers.html.description:
Salt traders who were mostly nomadic, serving as the intermediaries between the Mediterranean and the rest of the Sub-Saharan trade network.
They mainly just serve as another example of a group of people in Dar al-Islam trading.

period-1/pax-mongolica.html.title: Pax Mongolica
period-1/pax-mongolica.html.description: The Pax Mongolica was a period of peace under the Mongols in which the Mongols secured the Silk Road with the use of the Yam system. These relays helped secure the trade routes and made it much safer and cheaper to trade. Additionally, the expansion of the Mongol empire meant that many regions like Russia, China, Persia, and parts of Europe were now interconnected. Under the Pax Mongolica, trade was encouraged and incentivized, leading to the growth of trade across the giant Mongol empire. The state encouragement of trade and the deliberate securing of trade routes led to expanded networks of exchange. This peace was a MASSIVE change, due to the immense scale that the Mongols aimed to conduct trade at - never before had previously disconnected regions interacted at such a large scale. Before, ideas, culture, goods, and technology only moved through a single region, but after the Mongols connected these regions (Persia, Europe, china, etc), ideas, technology, etc. moved across regions (examples could be gunpowder moving further just China to Europe, compass, etc.)

China gained immense wealth by forcing other nations to pay tribute to them, in goods or money (goods like Champa Rice were accepted as well). They mainly had three major tributary states: Vietnam, Japan, and Korea. By forcing other states to pay tribute, China was able to maintain power and prevent the other states from accumulating too much wealth. The tribute system was based on the idea that China is the cultural, economic, and technological leader of Asia, and as long as tribute states kept paying tribute, that idea was perpetuated and legitimized China’s rule.
An effect of this was that China would sinicize other nations and cause them to change.
Korea would basically become a Chinese clone, adopting Confucianism entirely, and Neo-Confucianism was adopted by Japan and Korea. The Chinese Writing system was adopted by all three as well.

The Aztecs would use Hueztiloptochopqwpeipli as a religious justification and by enforcing a state religion, making it easier for them to govern the general population - without the promotion of this religion by the state as a whole, they could not justify their large military, slavery, and expanstion to the general population. This also gives them a sacred mission to legitimize the Aztec's rule.HueztilopothcliA social system where men and women aren’t necessarily better or worse than one another, they just work in separate fields. They aren’t superior or inferior but work different jobs, thus the “parallelism”: By allowing different genders to still work and have power, rulers can help mitigate rebellions and unrest due to inequality. Furthermore, gender parallelism makes work more efficient and is a way to form a division of labor, making a state more productive and thus legitimizing a state that can provide more for its people. Both the Aztecs and the Mongols had examples of gender parallelism, in the Mongols, women played a vital logistic role of setting up camps and travelling with men to war while men were the physical soldiers. The most significant part is that both genders, while working different roles and may still have their "spheres", have equal rights and are treated equally regardless.Gender ParallelismChina is important as the most complete and strongest nation during this time, and will be so until P3 (there's a lot of evidence that China, due to the silver market was the key producer of luxury goods) China is the biggest producer and exporter of luxury goods such as silk, porcelain, and tea, all highly valuable goods that were traded along the Silk Road - trade was driven by China and the demand for China's luxury goods. As trade expanded, so did demand for Chinese goods, especially in Europe. China, as a benefit of being highly advanced, also developed many leading technologies at the time that were also dispersed China
The Zheng He voyages, during the Ming empire, were voyages to establish trade relations that expanded China’s view of the world, but these voyages were stopped after Zheng He - China isolated itself (Due to Ming Xenophobia & confucianism). However, these voyages were extremely successful, going as far as many of the Swahili Trade States in East Africa.
The Ming dynasty was prosperous at this time and thus the voyages were extravagant, with ships of sizes never seen before. This demonstrated to their tributary states and their population how prosperous and powerful they were, intimidating and also convincing their population not to rebel due to their prosperity/strength. These voyages helped legitimize the Ming Dynasty, yet they were shut down due to confucianist elites (yes I know that's not a word) fearing that this extended view would lead to more radical views from their populationZheng He VoyagesWhile the Grand Canal itself was built and predated the Song dynasty, during the Song dynasty they extended the Canal and made a lot of general improvements to the grand canal, helping control floods and also increase agricultural productivity.
This helps China improve and helps them maintain their rule as this economic improvement helps stability. Combined with a highly commercialized economy, the increased Grand Canal allowed for trade and huge economic output, leading to the so-called Song Economic Revolution. All of this helped legitimize their government and helped them keep the Mandate Of HeavenGrand Canal
Champa rice was a strain of rice that allowed China's population to increase heavily, and it was gained by the tributary system in one of Vietnam's tributes.
Champa rice as a good has no other comparison, as it was a critical bulk food source. While other important crops are like “cash crops”, such as spices, the closest thing you can get to Champa rice, a good that actually directly improves living standards is Bananas (and other trade goods), yet Champa rice drove China's population so much more than bananas did for their respective states.
This helped increase China's productivity during this time, and increased economic wealth helped lead to a stable and happy population, legitimizing their rule, especially important to maintain the Mandate Of HeavenChampa Rice
China developed many methods in order to increase agricultural production, such as irrigation, terrace farming, and crop rotation. These methods helped its population explode and made farming very efficient, and would be transferred to the European states.
This helped China legitimize their rule is by increasing prosperity, since a prosperous nation is one that is less likely to have rebellions. China was able to be stable during the Song dynasty since there was a food surplus to power population growth without starvation. This stability allowed the Song dynasty to maintain its rule and their Mandate Of Heaven
Eventually, these techniques would spread throughout the SIlk Road, and by not having to live hand to mouth, Commercialization occured on the global scale, improving the entire world economy.Song Agri Techniques
India at this time period was still not complete and a set of separate states, some of which did control large amounts of territory, but the entire state didn't exist yet. The strongest out of these was probably the Dehli sultanate, important because it's ANOTHER example of the success of Sufis and the spread of Islam. India is also important because it's also technologically advanced and is a key exporter, similar to China - they developed many things such as the 0 number system, important for foundational mathematics...India
The strait of Malacca was a hugely important strategic strait that controlled the S.E asia trade route with China and India. Notably, the Malaccan Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate, demonstrating the reach of Islam during this time. They also demonstrate the extent of Sufis, Muslim missionaries, who brought Islam to the Spice Islands.
Similar to the Swahili Trade States, the Malaccan Sultanate was trade-based state, focusing on taxing trade through the strait in order to generate profit to fund its economy and military. The Malaccan Sultanate demonstrates that trade could become the basis of a nation as networks of exchange developed during this time period.Malaccan Sultanate
The so-called lands of the Zanj, city states like Mogadishu, Kilwa, and many other states. They are a clear example of trade based states! Similar to other nations such as the Mali Empire, the Muslim Berbers, Al-Andalus, the Malaccan Sultanate, etc. these states sponsored trade heavily to build their economies. Helped heavily to expand IOB trade to Africa and connect IOB trade to the Sub-Saharan trade network. They utilized trade heavily in order to survive, with the Zanj states being decentralized states that competed with each other for trade. With so much competition, trade increased even more as trade became more profitable as each state had to lower prices (supply and demand).Swahili Trade StatesAn institution that was created in the Abbasid Caliphate that greatly contributed to Dar al-Islam's dominance in technological development in the sciences. By preserving Greco-Roman knowledge, they also contributed heavily to the Renaissance, and helped preserve ancient Greco-Roman innovations along with developing new technological innovations. The House of Wisdom helped strengthen the Abbasid Caliphate by maintaining strong technological and cultural flourishing, and the successes and artistic flourishing of the Golden Age of Islam legitimized the Abbasid Caliphate even after political and military defeats.House of Wisdom
An important city-state and the heart of the Abbasid Caliphate, its sacking led to the death of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Similar to other major city states such as Timbuktu or Tenochitlan, Baghdad was vitally important as a cultural and trade hub in the Abbasid Caliphate and contributed much to its dominance - due to the House of Wisdom, it attracted scholars from different cultures and regions that greatly contributed to technological development and the prevalence of Dar al-Islam, examples include Avicenna and the Canon of Medicine or Nasir al-Din al-Tusi and geometry.Baghdad
The major ARAB caliphate, during its reign was the Golden Age of Islam. Slowly, it did lose power in the Middle east but the final blow was only when the Mongols arrived and sacked Baghdad, turning it into the il-Khanate. It supported many centers of cultural learning such as Baghdad and the House of Wisdom, and the growth of trade was supported by these cultural and intellectual hubs that attracted merchants. After their fall, they would be replaced by the Turks, but their influence in creating such strong cultural sites still remained, especially in the technologies that H.O.W scholars developed.Abbasid CaliphateIslam is one of the main Abrahamic religions, and is relatively recent. It has a lot of traits and terminology... as a basic rundown, Muslims believe in parts of other Abrahamic religions (Judaism and Christianity) but ultimately believe that these have diverged and hold their own holy scripture, the Quran, as truth. They follow the Sharia law, religious law that governs all aspects of law. This basic aspect of Muslim nations is part of what makes Islam appealing to adopt by leaders due to the strict laws it already has built into it.
Another important aspect of Muslims are the Sufis, religious missionaries, who are extremely important during this time to spread Muslim thought and Islam.IslamThe richest man during this time, mainly remembered for his expensive Hajj, where he made his pilgrimage to Mecca. It was ridiculously expensive, where Mansa Musa threw money and gold everywhere, additionally building Mosques, contributing to the spread of Islam in Africa. Mansa Musa was a devout Muslim, and after completing his Hajj, he was even more devout and essentially converted the entirety of the Mali empire into a Muslim state.
This Hajj helped legitimize the Mali Empire by demonstrating the immense wealth of the Mali empire. As a secondary goal, it also led to the spread of Islam in Africa as well, cementing African empires in the use of Islam to maintain power. Another effect of his hajj was the legends it created, leading Europeans to flock to Africa to try and find wealth (the Catalan Atlas and that one image)Mansa Musa
As a highly important cultural hotpost, it was developed after Mansa Musa's Hajj, extremely Muslim, and was a critical trade city. Similar to Baghdad, as a core cultural hotspot, it also served as an important place of learning.
Timbuktu also served a practical purpose of legitimizing the Mali Empire, by serving as a beacon of its strength and Muslim identity. Thus, it attracted many other traders from other nations justifying the Mali empire to both foreign and domestic peoples.TimbuktuThe Khanate of the Golden Horde was the name that the Russians gave to the Mongols, and controlled Russia. This Khanate allowed princes to be autonomous, and there was little impact politically and culturally - there was the least assimilation on the Mongol's part here. However, by letting princes be autonomous, they weakened their own control and made it easier to rebel as princes gained centralized power and gained wealth. Another effect of their actions by staying hands-off was the survival of the Eastern Orthodox Church, which they did NOT destroy.Golden HordeThe dynasty that the Mongols created in China, to legitimize themselves as rules while they extracted wealth from China - keep in mind this is a dynasty, they had to follow dynastic rules. In the Yuan Dynasty, the Mongols sinicized, and didn’t cause major changes to China - the Yuan dynasty adopted Confucian traditions, continuing typical Chinese dynastic traditions. They would follow many Confucian traditions in order to maintain their power and legitimize themselves, ultimately staying out of China. China changed the Mongols, but the Mongols didn’t change China.Yuan Dynasty
The Persian Khanate of the Mongols, after the sacking of Baghdad, they destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate and ended the Arab rule of Islam (leading to the rise of the Turks). The Mongols would assimilate into Muslim/Persian culture and adapt their customs to maintain their rule. The formation of the il-Khanate was significant and changed politics in the Middle East due to their ending of the Abassid caliphate and the ensuing power vacuum leading to the rise of the Turks in Islam, such as the Mamluks.il-KhanateThe replacement of the il-Khanate, VERY IMPORTANT as an example of a key development: Dar al-Islam turned from Arabs to Turks, and the Mamluks are a clear example of this. They were similar to the Mongols in strategy and originally started as slaves.Mamluk SultanateCommericialization is the change from a hand-to-mouth economy to one where prodiucers and farmers can produce excess and sell it for profit, turning teir job into one that mainly focuses on selling excess goods. This development occurred throughout most states during this time period, and was caused by increased prosperity due to spreading technologies which improved living conditions. However, this effect also occurred independently of trade, and commercialization would also become a cause of increased trade as producers sought to sell their goods to others. In general, any increase in living conditions would help lead to commercialization, which almost always improved the economy of a state and led to the rise of a middle class.Commercialization
Bananas were a new good from the land of the Zanj (Swahili Trade States) and were a new food that is evidence of widespread trade and interconnected global networks. They would eventually spread to mostly be grown in Southeast Asia and eventually, the Americas (but not yet).
Champa Rice was a valuable trade good that was gained in tribute and given to China, and is an example of how interconnected networks of trade led to increased economic production, and how important trade was to gain access to new goods.
Luxury goods (ivory, silk, porcelain) were also traded during this time period, and demand would increase dramatically. While luxury goods were a cause for new trade routes due to their high demand, the effect was that the producers of luxury goods would become extremely wealthy, like the gold nation of the Mali Empire or China.Bananas (and other trade goods)
Another key development during this time period was the creation of trade-based states, new nations appeared that utilized only trade to grow their wealth and power. These states demonstrate the impact of trade in P1, as nations grew to rely on trade. States such as the Mali Empire, Malaccan Sultanate, and the Swahili Trade States would all rely on trade as the basis for their economy. This led to a cyclical effect where as trade based states grew in power, they increased their trade output and thus grew the very networks they relied on. Development of Trade States
Trade, due to monsoon winds, would lead to diasporic merchant communities, as evidenced by intermarriage of merchants and communities in states like Kilwa/Swahili states that use Arabic + Bantu as their language. This intermixing of different cultures lead to Religious Syncretism and the spread of religion especially Islam (Dar al-Islam).Diasporic Merchant CommunitiesReligious syncretism, or the merging of religions, occurred often after the increase in trade and new networks as religions also spread.
Some examples include Mahayana Buddhism and Theravada Buddhism, with Theravada Buddhism being practiced in India and Mahayana in China. Another example is the Africanization of Islam and merging with local traditions, such as improved treatment of women inside the Mali Empire. In these cases, religions would transform as they passed through trade routes due to cross-regional interactions. Religious SyncretismA traveler who wrote accounts and travel literature, he extensively traveled across the IOB and his accounts were valuable to provide insight into life there. Other explorers and traders also sold travel literature in this time period, and increased knowledge of city states helped traders and travelers travel across dar Al-Islam, travel literature like the Rihala was useful to merchants and gave critical information they might need to travel. One of the effects and the causes of increased trade were new travelers, who were able to travel across the world and also act as merchants. These travelers disseminated travel information, but also used information from increased trade. Thus, these new travelers led to increased cross-regional interaction and also disseminated information about these new interactions.Ibn BattutaThe bubonic plague is a NEGATIVE effect that occured due to growing trade networks, and it was started at the Siege of Caffa by the Mongols applying biological warfare. It would kill and decimate Europe and the rest of the world due to poor sanitation, and would cause so much harm that 1/3 of the world population would die off. It was extremely destructive, and had global reach. Due to growing trade networks, the bubonic plague was able to spread much more rapidly and much further than previous epidemics. Without the growth of trade networks, and the movement of merchants, the bubonic plague would not have been able to spread as far.Bubonic PlagueTechnologies would disperse due to trade, such as paper (China), the concept of 0 (India), paper money, gunpowder, the compass, the astrolabe, the lateen sail, and many, many more technologies. These would eventually be Europe's ticket to modernization, and how they would colonize other nations as they got access to these new technologies and copied them with improvements. Technological Dispersion
Sufis and Sufism were extremely important during this time period due to their unparalled ability to spread Islam, even more than state methods and forced conversion. These Muslim religious missionaries would travel around and gain converts, and would appeal to local religions and be tolerant of local customs, partly why they were so successful (led to Religious Syncretism). They were also successful due to Islam having the Sharia, which made governance by rulers much easier and also was encouraging of trade which helped gain converts in trade states.
With improved trade networks, they were able to spread Islam to even places like India, the Swahili Trade States, and the Malaccan Sultanate. The improvement in trade networks helped improve their reach, showing how growing trade networks led to the spread of Islam.Sufis
As trade increased the wealth of nations, (Commercialization), these states would start to have hubs for trade and thus cities would start being created/developed to a larger extent. These new important cities led to a wealth of new opportunities, especially to support a new middle class with specific skills such as artists. Urbanization helped lead to new jobs and increased prosperity.UrbanizationThe gold empire of West Africa in this time period, replaced by  the Songhai empire BUT because of Mansa Musa’s efforts, their Muslim ideas were continued to the next empires, and thus Africa adopted Islam.
The Mali empire, similar to the idea of the Pax Mongolica, created a similar Pax Mandinka, where the Mali empire sponsored trade and allowed trade to flourish. Similar to the Yam System, the Mali empire deterred bandits and secured trade routes.Mali Empire
The Pax Mongolica was a period of peace under the Mongols in which the Mongols secured the Silk Road with the use of the Yam system. These relays helped secure the trade routes and made it much safer and cheaper to trade. Additionally, the expansion of the Mongol empire meant that many regions like Russia, China, Persia, and parts of Europe were now interconnected. Under the Pax Mongolica, trade was encouraged and incentivized, leading to the growth of trade across the giant Mongol empire. The state encouragement of trade and the deliberate securing of trade routes led to expanded networks of exchange. This peace was a MASSIVE change, due to the immense scale that the Mongols aimed to conduct trade at - never before had previously disconnected regions interacted at such a large scale. Before, ideas, culture, goods, and technology only moved through a single region, but after the Mongols connected these regions (Persia, Europe, china, etc), ideas, technology, etc. moved across regions (examples could be gunpowder moving further just China to Europe, compass, etc.)Pax MongolicaSalt traders who were mostly nomadic, serving as the intermediaries between the Mediterranean and the rest of the Sub-Saharan trade network. They mainly just serve as another example of a group of people in Dar al-Islam trading.Muslim BerbersSecure rest stops and inns that helped secure the trade routes, making long-distance trading much easier.
Similar to the Yam System, see for more details on why improved security helped
Another effect that occurred with the rise of caravanserai was that traders not only could rest and trade but they could also connect with one another, helping increase cross-cultural connections as merchants met in caravanserai (see people like Ibn Battuta)Caravanserai
Al-Andalus, by itself wasn't a huge power, but was the essential transfer point for how the ideas of Dar-al Islam transferred onto W. Europe, especially during the Crusades. Serving as this connection point, al-Andalus helped bring out ideas into the wider world and disperse goods & technology into Western Europe, helping create the basis for the Renaissance.Al-Andalus
The securing of the Mongol empire's trade routes, leading to the Pax Mongolica. These relays made it much safer and cheaper for trade to occur, and these relays helped with communication as well and governance over their massive empire. The relays did not govern trade, instead they secured the trade routes and only taxed merchants using their services slightly - by not controlling trade, and only taxing merchants, trade still occurred and the increased safety overweighed the cost. Thus, the Yam System, by securing trade routes, helped expand the use of trade networks and due to the size of the Mongol empire, helped facilitate cross-regional interactions.Yam System
The parallel of Divine Right of Kings in China, the mandate of heaven essentially stated that dynasties had the right to rule and also the right to be overthrown as soon as they became tyrannic. Thus, most dynasties would attempt to use legitimacy and not direct control in order to maintain power over their population, knowing that direct control would lead to a rebellion.
However, this also helped them, as with economic growth and a commercialized economy the Song would be able to legitimize themselves for centuries due to their economic revolution. Mandate Of Heaven
A belief system that heavily influenced chinese thinking, this is HUGE and contributes to the overall trajectory of China - HUGELY IMPORTANT!
Respecting one's elders: a fundamental part of Chinese and Confucianist society. Filial piety helped reinforce the 5 Relationships and the clear social structure of Chinese society, making it even more difficult to rebel and leave one's social class as it acts as a cultural tie to maintain social hierarchies.
The 5 key relationships: husband-wife, peer-peer, ruler-subject, father-son, elder-younger sibling, were core relationships and had clear superior inferior interactions in Confucianism. This helped create a social hierarchy, making it easier for a ruler to govern. Another effect is in gender roles, where it developed and created a patriarchal society which can be seen through acts such as Foot Binding.Meritocratic examinations that anyone could take, leading to the creation of a scholar-official bureaucratic class. However, it was still unbalanced and unfair due to tutoring, much like the SAT and other "standardized" exams today, rich families could afford the tutors necessary, but sometimes poor towns would sponsor someone. By allowing limited social ability, rulers gave commoners a way to improve their status keeping commoners more accepting of their lowered social status (they "deserved" it). However, the prohibitive cost also served to still keep commoners out of higher social levels, and helped keep the rulers in power.
The most important thing was that in BOTH the Ming and Song dynasties, Confucianism was a continuity. This continuity is important because it shows us how even in the face of the Mongols, China itself changed little to accommodate for them, culturally and socially, and the major changes were economic and political. The "heart" of China stayed the same.ConfucianismCamels were the primary beast of burden, and were necessary to traverse the Saharan desert due to their capacity to stay alive and not die, unlike horses. Without camels, it would be impossible to develop the sub-saharan trade networks to the scale they existed at, as large-scale trade would not be possible.Camels only became useful after the invention of camel-saddles, facilitating trade in the Sahara as they could finally be used as true beasts of burden to carry loads, not just people. Without the camel-saddle and the camel, large transactions, especially transactions of SALT, which was vital to the Saharan economy.
Knowledge of the Saharan environment and the existence of guides was also tremendously important, for example in the case of Ibn Battuta, we know that guides were common and widespread. Due to the harsh nature of the Sahara, it would have been suicide to attempt a crossing without knowledge of oasises and other intermediate destinations, and as knowledge was accumulated, it led to the growth of trade networks as merchants found it more safe to conduct business.Sub-Saharan Technological InnovationsThe DRoK was the justification of Kings during this time period, where they justified their rule by using God and Christianity as the justification, believing that God meant for this King to rule.
DRoK is an example of religion being used to control and maintain power by states, such as the Mandate Of Heaven, Blood Sacrifice, and Islam (Mansa Musa). This led to a cyclical relationship with the Church, while Kings had more political power technically the Pope (who could excommunicate) was more powerful as they could attack their justification. This becomes important later on in Period 2, with new branches of Christianity, as kingdoms adopt new branches to protect their justifications.Divine Right of Kings
The Catholic Church/Pope crowned kings/lords, and lords in feudalism needed to justify their rule by getting the permission of priests/pope (god, Divine Right of Kings). The Catholic Church and the feudal system justified their rule by using religion, due to heavy encouragement of Christianity in Europe - thus, with the support of the church, peasants would be less likely to rebel. The Crusades are an example of the Catholic Church legitimizing their rule, by attempting to adopt a religious mission and attempt to gain power and territory.Catholic ChurchThe only pure Aztec city-state, and the capital city of the Aztecs, which was also the only pure Aztec city-state. It was heavily urbanized and had large pyramids for blood sacrifice and religion. By controlling the most technologically advanced city, and enforcing migration due to payment of tactics, this would legitimize the Aztecs, showing how powerful and technologically advanced they were to neighboring states. By holding and controlling a single critical city, it becomes easier to control a nation by simply controlling the pressure point of the region.TenochitlanKnots utilized for administration and communication of information, a unique way to govern and administrate an empire. These knots were early record keeping and were highly used - the fact that they developed such an extensive system of record-keeping shows how much they valued administration.QuipuThe Incas created a huge mountain road system to increase the ease of communication across their vast empire, constructing one of the largest communication systems in one of the harshest environments to build roads in (interaction with environment). The Incan empire utilized this road system to great effect for military and also commercial use, and it played a crucial role in helping maintain central control over their empire.Mountain RoadsProfessional traveling merchants in the Aztec empire, they became a class with high status. They were state sponsored traders, but also operated as private traders.
As state sponsored traders, they would help promote trade and help tie the economy of the nature together. However, they also served a similar purpose to the Censorate, as spies for the government to maintain power.PohtecaA system of forced labor, where the Incans would take a labor tax in order to fund large building projects such as mountain roads/irrigation. This labor tax (corvee) would become a continuity in the Spanish empire, and helped the Incan empire maintain their power as it helped construct many large public projects, further developing their empire and also ensuring that their states remained loyal and governed under the central governmentMi'ta SystemThe Aztecs were the most powerful state in Central America during this time period, and were notable for their heavy use of their military. They were extremely militarized, with their justification and purpose being blood sacrifice funded by Flowery Wars. They would bring other nations under their empire through conquest, and they would claim tribute from conquered nations. While they didn't have as much cross city-state trade interaction, their capital Tenochtitlan was extremely modern, and they had already began commercialization and urbanization in Tenochtitlan.AztecsEssentially ancient socialists, the Incan empire was characterized by being peaceful and sharing resources communally, but still invading other territories. They would invite states to become tributary states and join their empire and would conquer them if they declined. They were extremely skilled at administration over their entire empire and created many great public works, increasing trade networks in south america.Incan
There's not much to say, as Europe was far, far behind all other states during this time. They would start catching up in P2 and eventually be ahead in P3/4, but for now they're a series of disconnected feudal states with no centralized rule. Italian trade city-states do exist, (Venice, Genoa) however, and are a great example of Development of Trade States in different regions. Europe did participate in world trade, and the silk road would lead to events in P2 - extreme demand for luxury goods and the desire to bypass middlemenEurope

headers.heading: Trade,  growing networks of exhange, and their effects are the most important things to know in P1!

period-1/period-1-home.html.title: Period 1 Home
period-1/period-1-home.html.description:
How to use Please do not use this as your primary study tool for the MCQs, you will get destroyed as a lot of the content is missing. If you can't tell things are missing you haven't studied your MKCs hard enough. Notice how the social structure of Confucianism is not mentioned... I am not jardworking enough to make a 100% complete list, unless y'all start paying me. Please do not rely on this for MCQs, this is primarily a study tool for a quick refresh/cram or to see how to connect things together on the FRQs The Period 1.canvas is just an organized mind map to see how all of the major topics fit together, starting with the major trade regions, then which states were in each trade region. If possible, how each nation justified their rule is also included as well, and then these states and trade networks all changed due to the influence of the Mongols (except for the Americas, they're special), and the effects of the changes the Mongols brought.
Period 1.canvas - a mind map for a quick refresher
Period 1 Resources - a list of most past SAQs related to ONLY this time period
Period 1 Contextualization - a list of things that happened before or during this time period to contextualize essay questions for Period 1 (it's not context in this time period for Period 2) The 1200-1450 was dominated by the effects of increasing trade There were many wide-reaching effects, such as the spread of religions, bubonic plague, increased inter-community interactions During this time period, Europe was far behind and Dar al-Islam/China were the major powers during this time The IOB was by far the most important trading area In the Americas, the Aztecs and Inca set up their own empires/states which operated to some extent similarly to the Afro-Eurasian empires The three major trade routes were the Sub-Saharan trade routes, the Silk Road, and the IOB (maritime) With rising trade, so did rising production of luxury goods from India and China that increased their power.

period-1/serfdom.html.title: Serfdom
period-1/serfdom.html.description:
Serfs could not leave the land of their lords, swearing fealty to their lords and were slave-like, different from peasants who were free
By keeping the lower classes’ power limited, European lords were able to maintain power and prevent rebellions. Serfs had such little power that they were generally extremely weak, allowing their lords to keep exploiting them. By forcing them to remain in the same location, they severely weakened the peasants' ability to congregate for rebellion - thus the social structure of serfdom and the need to swear fealty was a huge advantage for lords to maintain power.
