period-1/sufis.html.title: Sufis
period-1/sufis.html.description:
Sufis and Sufism were extremely important during this time period due to their unparalled ability to spread Islam, even more than state methods and forced conversion. These Muslim religious missionaries would travel around and gain converts, and would appeal to local religions and be tolerant of local customs, partly why they were so successful (led to Religious Syncretism). They were also successful due to Islam having the Sharia, which made governance by rulers much easier and also was encouraging of trade which helped gain converts in trade states.
With improved trade networks, they were able to spread Islam to even places like India, the Swahili Trade States, and the Malaccan Sultanate. The improvement in trade networks helped improve their reach, showing how growing trade networks led to the spread of Islam.

period-1/swahili-trade-states.html.title: Swahili Trade States
period-1/swahili-trade-states.html.description: The so-called lands of the Zanj, city states like Mogadishu, Kilwa, and many other states. They are a clear example of trade based states! Similar to other nations such as the Mali Empire, the Muslim Berbers, Al-Andalus, the Malaccan Sultanate, etc. these states sponsored trade heavily to build their economies. Helped heavily to expand IOB trade to Africa and connect IOB trade to the Sub-Saharan trade network. They utilized trade heavily in order to survive, with the Zanj states being decentralized states that competed with each other for trade. With so much competition, trade increased even more as trade became more profitable as each state had to lower prices (supply and demand).

period-1/technological-dispersion.html.title: Technological Dispersion
period-1/technological-dispersion.html.description: Technologies would disperse due to trade, such as paper (China), the concept of 0 (India), paper money, gunpowder, the compass, the astrolabe, the lateen sail, and many, many more technologies. These would eventually be Europe's ticket to modernization, and how they would colonize other nations as they got access to these new technologies and copied them with improvements.

period-1/tenochitlan.html.title: Tenochitlan
period-1/tenochitlan.html.description:
The only pure Aztec city-state, and the capital city of the Aztecs, which was also the only pure Aztec city-state. It was heavily urbanized and had large pyramids for blood sacrifice and religion.
By controlling the most technologically advanced city, and enforcing migration due to payment of tactics, this would legitimize the Aztecs, showing how powerful and technologically advanced they were to neighboring states. By holding and controlling a single critical city, it becomes easier to control a nation by simply controlling the pressure point of the region.

period-1/timbuktu.html.title: Timbuktu
period-1/timbuktu.html.description:
As a highly important cultural hotpost, it was developed after Mansa Musa's Hajj, extremely Muslim, and was a critical trade city. Similar to Baghdad, as a core cultural hotspot, it also served as an important place of learning.
Timbuktu also served a practical purpose of legitimizing the Mali Empire, by serving as a beacon of its strength and Muslim identity. Thus, it attracted many other traders from other nations justifying the Mali empire to both foreign and domestic peoples.

period-1/tribute-system.html.title: Tribute System
period-1/tribute-system.html.description:
China gained immense wealth by forcing other nations to pay tribute to them, in goods or money (goods like Champa Rice were accepted as well). They mainly had three major tributary states: Vietnam, Japan, and Korea. By forcing other states to pay tribute, China was able to maintain power and prevent the other states from accumulating too much wealth. The tribute system was based on the idea that China is the cultural, economic, and technological leader of Asia, and as long as tribute states kept paying tribute, that idea was perpetuated and legitimized China’s rule.
An effect of this was that China would sinicize other nations and cause them to change.
Korea would basically become a Chinese clone, adopting Confucianism entirely, and Neo-Confucianism was adopted by Japan and Korea. The Chinese Writing system was adopted by all three as well.
Furthermore, in Japan and Korea (Vietnam resisted), the role of women also shifted due to Confucianism, and these nations became more patriarchal.

period-1/urbanization.html.title: Urbanization
period-1/urbanization.html.description: As trade increased the wealth of nations, (Commercialization), these states would start to have hubs for trade and thus cities would start being created/developed to a larger extent. These new important cities led to a wealth of new opportunities, especially to support a new middle class with specific skills such as artists. Urbanization helped lead to new jobs and increased prosperity.

period-1/yam-system.html.title: Yam System
period-1/yam-system.html.description: The securing of the Mongol empire's trade routes, leading to the Pax Mongolica. These relays made it much safer and cheaper for trade to occur, and these relays helped with communication as well and governance over their massive empire. The relays did not govern trade, instead they secured the trade routes and only taxed merchants using their services slightly - by not controlling trade, and only taxing merchants, trade still occurred and the increased safety overweighed the cost. Thus, the Yam System, by securing trade routes, helped expand the use of trade networks and due to the size of the Mongol empire, helped facilitate cross-regional interactions.

period-1/yuan-dynasty.html.title: Yuan Dynasty
period-1/yuan-dynasty.html.description:
The dynasty that the Mongols created in China, to legitimize themselves as rules while they extracted wealth from China - keep in mind this is a dynasty, they had to follow dynastic rules.
In the Yuan Dynasty, the Mongols sinicized, and didn’t cause major changes to China - the Yuan dynasty adopted Confucian traditions, continuing typical Chinese dynastic traditions. They would follow many Confucian traditions in order to maintain their power and legitimize themselves, ultimately staying out of China. China changed the Mongols, but the Mongols didn’t change China.

period-1/zheng-he-voyages.html.title: Zheng He Voyages
period-1/zheng-he-voyages.html.description:
The Zheng He voyages, during the Ming empire, were voyages to establish trade relations that expanded China’s view of the world, but these voyages were stopped after Zheng He - China isolated itself (Due to Ming Xenophobia & confucianism). However, these voyages were extremely successful, going as far as many of the Swahili Trade States in East Africa.
The Ming dynasty was prosperous at this time and thus the voyages were extravagant, with ships of sizes never seen before. This demonstrated to their tributary states and their population how prosperous and powerful they were, intimidating and also convincing their population not to rebel due to their prosperity/strength. These voyages helped legitimize the Ming Dynasty, yet they were shut down due to confucianist elites (yes I know that's not a word) fearing that this extended view would lead to more radical views from their population

period-2/akbar-the-great.html.title: Akbar the Great
period-2/akbar-the-great.html.description:
One of the greatest Mughal kings, Akbar the Great was extremely religiously tolerant, with a policy of sulh i kul (no, you don't need to know that) a belief system/way of life of acceptance of religions. Some notable law changes were that Shia/Sunni were allowed to both worship in the same mosques, and the abolishment of the Jizya tax completely.
By promoting religious diversity, Akbar limited rebellions due to the majority Hindu population in the Mughal empire, which would have rose up in rebellion under religious limitations from foreigners. These policies led to India becoming a religious hotspot, allowing for religions to mix and spread, such as Sikhism, a merger between Hinduism and Muslim ideas. Without religious tolerance during this period, these cultures would have been unable to mix.

period-2/amsterdam-stock-exchange.html.title: Amsterdam Stock Exchange
period-2/amsterdam-stock-exchange.html.description:
The Amsterdam Stock Exchange created an environment for these joint stock companies to survive - they provided opportunities for common people to invest in these companies, giving them more wealth to be able to conduct business. 
Furthermore, due to the prior existence of the stock exchange, people were already used to investing stock into normal companies, and eventually buying stock into trade voyages would become common as well. Thus, this environment fostered investment into these companies, making the VOC the first companies to successfully achieve a monopoly as a joint stock company (even though it was established after the EIC)

period-2/battle-of-chaldiran.html.title: Battle of Chaldiran
period-2/battle-of-chaldiran.html.description: A battle between the Safavid and the Ottoman empires, which the Ottomans won. This is an example of the religious tension and religious wars due to Sunni vs. Shiites, and is an easy example of clear, direct fighting between these empires. The Ottomans would heavily utilized Gunpowder in order to defeat the Safavid with superior cannon technology. Thus, this battle shows how vital gunpowder was to expansion and conflict between these new empires.

period-2/battle-of-panipat.html.title: Battle of Panipat
period-2/battle-of-panipat.html.description: Similar to the Battle of Chaldiran, an example of how important gunpowder was, without gunpowder, the Delhi Sultanate would collapse under the pressure of the new Mughal empire where the Mughals utilized gunpowder heavily to dominate the Dehli Sultanate.

period-2/black-pepper.html.title: Black Pepper
period-2/black-pepper.html.description: A monopoly on black pepper by the Portuguese, it was one of the major monopolies of the IOB similar to nutmeg and the Dutch. This is a huge change: before, IOB trade was unrestricted and followed the concept of “free trade”, with independent trade-based city-states participating in trade. Monopolies limited and hindered free trade, and changed the I.O.B by introducing economic limitations. This leads to the Portuguese being able to become extremely rich and wealthy and profit off of the pepper trade, helping them catch up to China and the empires in the Middle East.

period-2/british-empire.html.title: British Empire
period-2/british-empire.html.description: The British empire would begin to emerge as a major power in this time period, but was still weaker than the other two dominant nations at the time: Spain and Portugal. They would have major holdings in India and the American colonies, which produced cotton/indigo and tobacco respectively, and would also be one of the first nations to adopt Joint Stock Companies in order to control their colonial holdings. They would only really develop during period 3 when they utilized Opium to gain immense wealth and had major control of China.

period-2/cartaz.html.title: Cartaz
period-2/cartaz.html.description: The trading post empire of the Portuguese was how they “colonized” and controlled the Indian Ocean Basin trade. By forcing ships to purchase a Cartaz, a document stating they were “allowed to trade”, they made money off of the I.O.B. Without one, ships couldn’t trade at their trading ports, and the Portuguese would act as privateers and raid ships. This system would slightly decrease trade in the I.O.B and changed the I.O.B from a "free-trade" system into a more controlled one. However, trade still continued, just with the Portuguese leeching off of its wealth.

period-2/devisherme.html.title: Devisherme
period-2/devisherme.html.description: A system in which young Christian boys were forcibly converted to become Janissaries (converted to become Muslims, and members of the officer class). They could become the elites of Ottoman society, and were a critical part of Ottoman administration. By having a class of people who were indoctrinated in loyalty to the Ottoman empire, who had no other loyalties, the Ottoman empire had a strong system of elites who were able to administrate and manage their empire efficiently without falling victim to power struggles. Furthermore, they were promoted mainly on meritocratic basis, as birth had no effect. Similar to the Mansabdars, this system helped create a meritocracy and helped the ruler maintain direct rule.

period-2/dutch.html.title: Dutch
period-2/dutch.html.description:
The Dutch would utilize their more developed economic and trade systems to accumulate wealth and capital and would also begin their imperial foray.
They would mainly control the Spice Islands, and gain immense wealth by forcibly turning the Banda islands into an export economy. This model would be used in period 3 and period 4 of converting colonies into export colonies, and it was extremely successful to gain immense wealth.

period-2/ghulam.html.title: Ghulam
period-2/ghulam.html.description: The Ghulam system was a system in which prisoners of war would be enslaved and used as slave soldiers. This provided an elite military force, and similar to the Mamluks, while they were slaves they were still paid highly and treated well in order to prevent rebellion. They served a similar role to Mamluks and Janissaries, where like in the Devisherme system these soldiers would be indoctrinated in loyalty to the Shah. This gave the Safavid empire one of the first examples of a trained military force, and helped maintain power by keeping the military under direct control of the ruler, and ensured that the military wasn't loyal to aristocratic families

period-2/gold,-god,-glory.html.title: Gold, God, Glory
period-2/gold,-god,-glory.html.description: These were the main motivations for Europeans to engage in maritime trade and develop new maritime empires.Gain money by bypassing Italian and Ottoman middlemen on land-based trade routes, especially for Chinese luxury goods which were still in high demand. Furthermore, find colonies for cash crops/silver/gold, which would also increase the wealth of these new empires.The failure of the crusades and the rise of Muslim empires threatened Christian nation-states and the church, which slowly had less money from tithes due to reduced population believing in the Catholic Church. Furthermore, the Protestant revolution severely weakened the Catholic Church, and Catholic states such as Spain were eager to find new targets to convert to Christianity.With Europe finally become more stabilized and the rise of stronger nation-states that had more direct political control over their governments and nobles, these rising nation states competed against each other for power. Having more colonies meant having more resources and more territory, which helped in the competition between these states

period-2/gunpowder.html.title: Gunpowder
period-2/gunpowder.html.description:
Possibly the single most important military advancement, allowed for the rise of new gunpowder empires which utilized gunpowder heavily in military conquests for expansion.
Gunpowder was imported from China into many other nations, but these nations would innovate heavily and gunpowder technology would determine the winners and losers of P2.

period-2/hacienda-+-encomienda.html.title: Hacienda + Encomienda
period-2/hacienda-+-encomienda.html.description: The hacienda/encomienda systems were systems in which Spain would force labor from indigenous people, supported by their caste system for their plantations and also silver mines. While the Mi’ta system was monthly and a labor tax, the encomienda and hacienda systems were abused systems of slavery, disguised as indentured servitude.

period-2/hall-of-mirrors.html.title: Hall of Mirrors
period-2/hall-of-mirrors.html.description:
A hall created to showcase the power of the Sun King, Louis XIV, and was in the grandiose and extravagant Palace of Versailles.
It's opulence put France into debt, which would eventually lead to many issues later down the line in the French Revolution, but when it was used in the Golden Age of France, it was used for meetings to intimidate nobles and legitimize Louis XIV by demonstrating his immense wealth.

period-2/imperial-examinations.html.title: Imperial Examinations
period-2/imperial-examinations.html.description: These imperial examinations continued from the past, from period 1 (A continuity!). The core governing system of the Qing was still Confucianism, which obviously continued to have similar benefits. Quote from other note: These were meritocratic examinations that anyone could take, leading to the creation of a scholar-official bureaucratic class. However, it was still unbalanced and unfair due to tutoring, much like the SAT and other "standardized" exams today, rich families could afford the tutors necessary, but sometimes poor towns would sponsor someone. By allowing limited social ability, rulers gave commoners a way to improve their status keeping commoners more accepting of their lowered social status (they "deserved" it). However, the prohibitive cost also served to still keep commoners out of higher social levels, and helped keep the rulers in power.

period-2/joint-stock-companies.html.title: Joint Stock Companies
period-2/joint-stock-companies.html.description:
Joint stock companies allowed for more risks to be taken across trade journeys due to distributed capital reducing the risk. Two major ones were created during P2, the British and the Dutch:The VOC and Dutch West Indies Co. gained enough capital to field a military and were able to drive away European competitors in the area like the British. They took over the Spice Islands, especially the Banda islands (due to nutmeg, and caused a genocide there). They leveraged their wealth to essentially operate as an independent nation, where they operated without any oversight.
Due to their profit-seeking nature, they would commit multiple crimes against humanity and use their immense wealth to control the Spice islands, esp. Nutmeg, for most of this period.
Their monopoly over spice was unseen before in the IOB, and would lead to other nations wanting in like the British which would try to take India. However there was a change from previous ideals - instead of shipping nutmeg straight back to Europe, they would sell within the IOB itself for valuable goods first, starting to participate in the lucrative IOB trade which is  a change as previously mercantilist policies led to colonies always shipping back to Europe.The British East India Co. was founded by the British in P.2 BEFORE the Dutch, and they would leverage their wealth to try and take over India. Using their immense wealth and status as a corporation without any state leverage, they quickly made alliances with people against the Mughals and then betrayed them once the Mughal threat was over.
They would then colonize and rule over India, chasing profit ruthlessly through activities such as growing opium, and building multiple trade monopolies and controlling the majority of the shipping back to Britain of luxury Indian and Chinese goods. (also basic commodities like saltpeter).

period-2/kangxi.html.title: Kangxi
period-2/kangxi.html.description: Kangxi would reemphasize Confucianism and turns China inward, only allowing maritime trade in 4 ports. Similar to the Tokugawa Shogunate, it is another example of resistance towards imperial expansion of Europeans by denying them access to their country.

period-2/manila-silver-galleons.html.title: Manila Silver Galleons
period-2/manila-silver-galleons.html.description: A key port in the Phillipines, the Spanish would arrive in Manila and ship silver from Potosi & Huancavelica and carry back luxury goods like porcelain/tea from China, utilizing the new technology of galleons that were able to make longer-distance trips with high amounts of cargo space. The Manila galleons show a change as they show how trade was truly globalized, finally including the Americas inside major trade routes.

period-2/mansabdars.html.title: Mansabdars
period-2/mansabdars.html.description:
The Mansabdars were an elite that was granted tax collection rights by the Mughal king, similar to lesser nobles in the European structure, where these nobles also had to support a military for the Mughal emperors. However, these titles could be taken away, and were not inherited.
This allowed for direct rule by the ruler, and this direct control limited rebellion and promoted stability by limiting the power of the aristocracy.
However, this system would still end up backfiring as the Mansabdars would build up immense power and wealth leading to splinter factions breaking up the Mughal empire.

period-2/maroon-societies.html.title: Maroon Societies
period-2/maroon-societies.html.description:
Slaves would attempt to escape, and they would form new societies that would continue resist slavery militarily directly and act as independent states (political/violent resistance).
However, they would also maintain their culture and preserve African culture in the Americas which was a form of cultural resistance against assimilation as well. Eventually, they would reintegrate into American society and help develop African-American culture.

period-2/millet-system.html.title: Millet System
period-2/millet-system.html.description:
A system where the Ottomans allowed subcommunities to self-govern, such as the Jews, Armenians, and Christians. They allowed them to govern themselves internally, effectively acting as a government within a government.
This religious tolerance meant that when the Ottomans conquered a region, instead of wasting time putting down uprisings due to an unwilling population, they could continue expanding rapidly. This allowed them to expand the reach of their territory tremendously and lowered the risk of rebellions internally from conquered peoples.
The Millet system allowed for many other nation-states to join the Ottoman empire and allowed them to quickly garner support from conquered nations. This led to extremely stable rule in the Ottoman empire, making the Ottoman empire one of the most stable and prosperous land-based empires, developing into the “Ottoman threat” to Europe.

period-2/missionary-activities.html.title: Missionary Activities
period-2/missionary-activities.html.description:
The Catholic Church would attempt to expand their influence in response to the Protestant reformation, especially through the use of Jesuit missionaries.
The Jesuits would be forced to adapt their teachings and scriptures to other cultures, leading to religious syncretism (e.g in China, Spanish Colonies, Pachamama).
Especially in the Spanish colonies, they were extremely prevalent and would focus on conversion of native peoples, relying on syncretism to carry their message across.

period-2/mughal-empire.html.title: Mughal Empire
period-2/mughal-empire.html.description: They would defeat the Delhi sultanate in the Battle of Panipat, and were especially famous for their religious tolerance. Under the rule of religiously tolerant leaders such as Akbar the Great, they would develop new religions such as Sikhism, but their final rulers such as Aurangzeb would be religiously intolerant and lead to the fall of the Mughals. They were important culturally, leading to Muslim rule in Hindu India and thus the aforementioned syncretism, and created pieces of monumental architecture such as the Taj Mahal.

period-2/nutmeg.html.title: Nutmeg
period-2/nutmeg.html.description: Similar to Black Pepper, nutmeg was another monopoly created by the dutch which dramatically shifted I.O.B trade: This is a huge change: before, IOB trade was unrestricted and followed the concept of “free trade”, with independent trade-based city-states participating in trade. Monopolies limited and hindered free trade, and changed the I.O.B by introducing economic limitations. Furthermore, the specific actions that the Dutch took were unique as they converted the Banda islands into an export economy, and in doing so created a genocide via starvation - a negative effect of increased trade.

period-2/ottoman-empire.html.title: Ottoman Empire
period-2/ottoman-empire.html.description: One of the other great powers, only behind the Qing empire (but definitely extremely powerful militarily). Due to its amazing geographic position, they acted as middlemen for Silk Road trade to Italian city states in the Mediterranean. Their markups were what caused Portugal and Spain to seek new sea routes to China for their ever-increasing desire of Chinese luxury goods. They would utilize a strong administration and military system to completely dominate all their neighbors, especially the Safavid empire in the Battle of Chaldiran as a Sunni empire.

period-2/period-2.html.description:

Thus, this also allowed the Tsar to gain a highly skilled administrative structure. The Millet system allowed for many other nation-states to join the Ottoman empire and allowed them to quickly garner support from conquered nations. This led to extremely stable rule in the Ottoman empire, making the Ottoman empire one of the most stable and prosperous land-based empires, developing into the “Ottoman threat” to Europe.Millet System
A battle between the Safavid and the Ottoman empires, which the Ottomans won. This is an example of the religious tension and religious wars due to Sunni vs. Shiites, and is an easy example of clear, direct fighting between these empires. The Ottomans would heavily utilized Gunpowder in order to defeat the Safavid with superior cannon technology. Thus, this battle shows how vital gunpowder was to expansion and conflict between these new empires.Battle of Chaldiran
Similar to the Battle of Chaldiran, an example of how important gunpowder was, without gunpowder, the Delhi Sultanate would collapse under the pressure of the new Mughal empire where the Mughals utilized gunpowder heavily to dominate the Dehli Sultanate.Battle of PanipatTaj Mahal was a huge mausoleum, being built at huge cost at the height of the Mughal empire under Shah Jahan. The immense Taj Mahal legitimized the Mughal empire by demonstrating their immense wealth and prosperity to their population, showing how prosperous the empire was. It also demonstrates religious syncretism, as it merges both Persian (read: Muslim) and Indian elements of architecture.Taj MahalA hall created to showcase the power of the Sun King, Louis XIV, and was in the grandiose and extravagant Palace of Versailles. It's opulence put France into debt, which would eventually lead to many issues later down the line in the French Revolution, but when it was used in the Golden Age of France, it was used for meetings to intimidate nobles and legitimize Louis XIV by demonstrating his immense wealth.Hall of MirrorsThe Sakoku edict of 1635 was an example of resistance to European imperialist expansion, as Japan closes themselves off to foreigners completely (except for the less-religious Dutch). This edict is an example of resistance because it shows Asian countries preventing Europeans from enroaching onto their territory, and demonstrates how European powers are not powerful enough to force the Eastern nations directly.Tokugawa Shogunate
A monopoly on black pepper by the Portuguese, it was one of the major monopolies of the IOB similar to nutmeg and the Dutch. This is a huge change: before, IOB trade was unrestricted and followed the concept of “free trade”, with independent trade-based city-states participating in trade. Monopolies limited and hindered free trade, and changed the I.O.B by introducing economic limitations. This leads to the Portuguese being able to become extremely rich and wealthy and profit off of the pepper trade, helping them catch up to China and the empires in the Middle East.Black Pepper
Similar to Black Pepper, nutmeg was another monopoly created by the dutch which dramatically shifted I.O.B trade: This is a huge change: before, IOB trade was unrestricted and followed the concept of “free trade”, with independent trade-based city-states participating in trade. Monopolies limited and hindered free trade, and changed the I.O.B by introducing economic limitations. Furthermore, the specific actions that the Dutch took were unique as they converted the Banda islands into an export economy, and in doing so created a genocide via starvation - a negative effect of increased trade.Nutmeg
The trading post empire of the Portuguese was how they “colonized” and controlled the Indian Ocean Basin trade. By forcing ships to purchase a Cartaz, a document stating they were “allowed to trade”, they made money off of the I.O.B. Without one, ships couldn’t trade at their trading ports, and the Portuguese would act as privateers and raid ships. This system would slightly decrease trade in the I.O.B and changed the I.O.B from a "free-trade" system into a more controlled one. However, trade still continued, just with the Portuguese leeching off of its wealth.CartazJoint stock companies allowed for more risks to be taken across trade journeys due to distributed capital reducing the risk. Two major ones were created during P2, the British and the Dutch:The VOC and Dutch West Indies Co. gained enough capital to field a military and were able to drive away European competitors in the area like the British. They took over the Spice Islands, especially the Banda islands (due to nutmeg, and caused a genocide there). They leveraged their wealth to essentially operate as an independent nation, where they operated without any oversight. Due to their profit-seeking nature, they would commit multiple crimes against humanity and use their immense wealth to control the Spice islands, esp. Nutmeg, for most of this period. Their monopoly over spice was unseen before in the IOB, and would lead to other nations wanting in like the British which would try to take India. However there was a change from previous ideals - instead of shipping nutmeg straight back to Europe, they would sell within the IOB itself for valuable goods first, starting to participate in the lucrative IOB trade which is  a change as previously mercantilist policies led to colonies always shipping back to Europe.The British East India Co. was founded by the British in P.2 BEFORE the Dutch, and they would leverage their wealth to try and take over India. Using their immense wealth and status as a corporation without any state leverage, they quickly made alliances with people against the Mughals and then betrayed them once the Mughal threat was over. They would then colonize and rule over India, chasing profit ruthlessly through activities such as growing opium, and building multiple trade monopolies and controlling the majority of the shipping back to Britain of luxury Indian and Chinese goods. (also basic commodities like saltpeter).Joint Stock CompaniesKangxi would reemphasize Confucianism and turns China inward, only allowing maritime trade in 4 ports.
Similar to the Tokugawa Shogunate, it is another example of resistance towards imperial expansion of Europeans by denying them access to their country.KangxiA revolution by the indigenous population in the Spanish colonies in New Mexico, who actually successfully drove out the Spanish. It was caused due to forced religious assimilation, and aimed to regain their religious freedoms. Ultimately, the Spanish returned when the Pueblo people started to run out of food, but the threat of revolt caused them to ease some of the religious restrictions they had previously imposed.Pueblo RevoltsRuler of the Ndongo and Matmba nations, she would negotiate treaties with the Portuguese and avoided turning the Ndongo and Matamba into other slave nations, fighting and rebelling against the Portuguese throughout her rule. She is a key example of the African resistance to the slave trade/European colonial expansion, and shows that not every single nation devolved into slavery in Africa in order to win the arms race against other African slave states, with some African nations resisting European influence.Queen Ana NzingaSlaves would attempt to escape, and they would form new societies that would continue resist slavery militarily directly and act as independent states (political/violent resistance). However, they would also maintain their culture and preserve African culture in the Americas which was a form of cultural resistance against assimilation as well. Eventually, they would reintegrate into American society and help develop African-American culture.Maroon SocietiesThe largest slave rebellion in the British colonies, in South Carolina slaves aimed to collect arms and march to Florida where the Spanish offered them freedom. Ultimately, the rebellion was put down and led to harsher restrictions, but is still an example of attempted resistance against the British empire.Stono RebellionThe Dutch would utilize their more developed economic and trade systems to accumulate wealth and capital and would also begin their imperial foray. They would mainly control the Spice Islands, and gain immense wealth by forcibly turning the Banda islands into an export economy. This model would be used in period 3 and period 4 of converting colonies into export colonies, and it was extremely successful to gain immense wealth.DutchThe British empire would begin to emerge as a major power in this time period, but was still weaker than the other two dominant nations at the time: Spain and Portugal.
They would have major holdings in India and the American colonies, which produced cotton/indigo and tobacco respectively, and would also be one of the first nations to adopt Joint Stock Companies in order to control their colonial holdings. They would only really develop during period 3 when they utilized Opium to gain immense wealth and had major control of China.British EmpireThe most powerful maritime empire in period 2, but significantly declined afterwards. They had most of their colonial holdings in the Americas, notably the Caribbean and Latin America. They would produce many cash crops such as sugar, coffee, cacao, but none of these were really as important as silver. Spain is important for introducing one of the biggest sources of American silver, and by trading it to China, would finally create a global trading network. However, they over relied on silver, and when inflation hit, their wealth would suddenly collapse especially due to a lack of diversification and economic development due to multiple wars.Spain
A major player in period 2, similar to Spain dramatically decreased in power after period 2. They would focus primarily on the I.O.B trade, interfering via the Cartaz system and would simply leech off of I.O.B trade and get incredibly wealthy. They would also have some holdings on the Malabr Coast and have a monopoly on the black pepper trade. Both of these were an important change to I.O.B trade, militarizing and turning previously free trade into a profit-seeking enterprise.PortugalThese were the main motivations for Europeans to engage in maritime trade and develop new maritime empires.Gain money by bypassing Italian and Ottoman middlemen on land-based trade routes, especially for Chinese luxury goods which were still in high demand. Furthermore, find colonies for cash crops/silver/gold, which would also increase the wealth of these new empires.The failure of the crusades and the rise of Muslim empires threatened Christian nation-states and the church, which slowly had less money from tithes due to reduced population believing in the Catholic Church. Furthermore, the Protestant revolution severely weakened the Catholic Church, and Catholic states such as Spain were eager to find new targets to convert to Christianity.With Europe finally become more stabilized and the rise of stronger nation-states that had more direct political control over their governments and nobles, these rising nation states competed against each other for power. Having more colonies meant having more resources and more territory, which helped in the competition between these statesGold, God, GloryDue to the influence of Dar al-Islam in P1, bringing in influence through Al-Andalus and the Crusades, Europe was undergoing a period of intellectual flourishing that encouraged technological development. This helped Europe adapt quickly to new maritime technologies that helped navigation and helped Europe catch up the the East. Furthermore, the environment of discovery and intellectual development led to many explorers and "discoverers", which led to more voyages by nations to help create maritime empires.RenaissanceThe Amsterdam Stock Exchange created an environment for these joint stock companies to survive - they provided opportunities for common people to invest in these companies, giving them more wealth to be able to conduct business.  Furthermore, due to the prior existence of the stock exchange, people were already used to investing stock into normal companies, and eventually buying stock into trade voyages would become common as well. Thus, this environment fostered investment into these companies, making the VOC the first companies to successfully achieve a monopoly as a joint stock company (even though it was established after the EIC)Amsterdam Stock ExchangeDid not actually explore, but instead was a state sponsor for explorers for Portugal, such as Vasco Da Gama's voyages for the Portuguese. He contributed capital and economic incentive for explorers, which was critical for developing motivation to explore and find new trade routes. This directly led to the development of new colonies and maritime empires, and Prince Henry and other state sponsors were vital to provide incentive to explorers to discover paths to new colonies.Prince Henry the NavigatorThe Catholic Church would attempt to expand their influence in response to the Protestant reformation, especially through the use of Jesuit missionaries. The Jesuits would be forced to adapt their teachings and scriptures to other cultures, leading to religious syncretism (e.g in China, Spanish Colonies, Pachamama). Especially in the Spanish colonies, they were extremely prevalent and would focus on conversion of native peoples, relying on syncretism to carry their message across.Missionary ActivitiesThe creation of new social structures in order to justify the Spanish use of the indigenous population, it was promoted heavily by the Spanish settlers. It was extremely detailed and government structured, with more and more indigenous blood being classified as lower class, and provided a racist and social justification for abuse of workers. As the colonies became more diverse, especially with African slaves, the social structure became increasingly more complex in order to continue supporting the whites/Spanish at the top of the social hierarchy. This system was created mainly in part due to the outnumbered Spanish: at 20% of the population, they were outnumbered and needed this clear social structure to limit the power of other groups and to prevent potential uprisings/rebellions if they gained too much power.**Sociedad de CastasThe hacienda/encomienda systems were systems in which Spain would force labor from indigenous people, supported by their caste system for their plantations and also silver mines. While the Mi’ta system was monthly and a labor tax, the encomienda and hacienda systems were abused systems of slavery, disguised as indentured servitude.Hacienda + Encomienda
A key port in the Phillipines, the Spanish would arrive in Manila and ship silver from Potosi & Huancavelica and carry back luxury goods like porcelain/tea from China, utilizing the new technology of galleons that were able to make longer-distance trips with high amounts of cargo space. The Manila galleons show a change as they show how trade was truly globalized, finally including the Americas inside major trade routes.Manila Silver GalleonsMt. Potosi and Mt Huancavelica were the main Spanish silver sources and were a core part of the Spanish colonies’ silver economy. Potosi was a literal mountain of silver, which allowed the Spanish to get rich off of the insatiable need for silver of the Qing. Mt. Potosi was the source of silver, and Huancavelica was the mercury hub that purified the silver. They heavily used forced labor/slave labor, and the toxic mercury was extremely dangerous leading to continuous importation of slaves.Potosi & HuancavelicaThe very existence of transatlantic trade was a huge change, with two major effects - the Columbian exchange and the Atlantic slave tradeThe columbian exchange was an exchange of foods and other species between Afro-Eurasia and the Americas, and was a biological exchange that occurred along with trade and voyages. The impact on Afro-Eurasia was a lot of food and crops that became commonplace - these crops could also grow in Afro-Eurasia, e.g potatoes in Ireland which became extremely commonplace and a huge source of daily calories (cacao is another example that becomes commonplace later.)  There are two key impacts on the Americas: first, the Great Dying, killing of the native populations leaving them ripe for colonization. Second, the importation of cash crops to the Americas led to the colonies becoming profitable and leading to a vicious cycle, and caused the rise of plantations and exploitation of the Americas due to cash crops being able to be grown there well.The slave trade also began with the rise of plantations which demanded cash crops. The plantation structure was created by the Arabs to farm sugar, and was copied by Europeans on any production economy that they colonized and created. This had a huge change on Africa, which was a major source of slaves - African nations would become funded and financed solely based on the slave trade, and even peaceful nations needed to participate (some exceptions) in order to get access to European arms to defend themselves. This left them high and dry when the Europeans abolished slavery and contributed to the long-term issues in Africa we see today.Transatlantic Trade

period-2/period-2-home.html.title: Period 2 Home
period-2/period-2-home.html.description:
How to use Please do not use this as your primary study tool for the MCQs, you will get destroyed as a lot of the content is missing. If you can't tell things are missing you haven't studied your MKCs hard enough. Again, there are missing things from the MKCs that are important such as more detail on examples of religious syncretism and minor gunpowder states such as Morrocco. I am not jardworking enough to make a 100% complete list, unless y'all start paying me. Please do not rely on this for MCQs, this is primarily a study tool for a quick refresh/cram or to see how to connect things together on the FRQs The Period 2.canvas is just an organized mind map to see how all of the major topics fit together, starting with the major trade regions, then which states were in each trade region. If possible, how each nation justified their rule is also included as well, and then these states and trade networks all changed due to the influence of the Mongols (except for the Americas, they're special), and the effects of the changes the Mongols brought.
Period 2.canvas - a mind map for a quick refresher
Period 2 Resources - a list of most past SAQs/LEQs/DBQs related to ONLY this time period
Period 2 Contextualization - a list of things that happened before or during this time period to contextualize essay questions for Period 1 (it's not context in this time period for Period 2) Major land-based empires would begin to form utilizing gunpowder technology Due to their increased size, they were forced to develop new economic, social, and religious systems to control their population The Renaissance would begin in Europe, allowing them to catch up technologically Europe began to catch up and develop their own maritime empires after unifying their independent nation-states The risks associated with maritime travel would lead to new economic methods to fund voyages Qing China would remain the single most important nation, and drive global trade with it's demand for silver As European nations colonized regions, they would bring a variety of changes to nations they conquered/colonized European nations would discover the Americas, and bring them into contact with the rest of the world, leading to truly global trading networks of exchange Belief systems would undergo dramatic changes under the stresses of new empires, with major divisions and infighting such as Protestant vs. Catholic and Sunni vs. Shia

period-2/portugal.html.title: Portugal
period-2/portugal.html.description: A major player in period 2, similar to Spain dramatically decreased in power after period 2. They would focus primarily on the I.O.B trade, interfering via the Cartaz system and would simply leech off of I.O.B trade and get incredibly wealthy. They would also have some holdings on the Malabr Coast and have a monopoly on the black pepper trade. Both of these were an important change to I.O.B trade, militarizing and turning previously free trade into a profit-seeking enterprise.

period-2/potosi-&-huancavelica.html.title: Potosi & Huancavelica
period-2/potosi-&-huancavelica.html.description:
Mt. Potosi and Mt Huancavelica were the main Spanish silver sources and were a core part of the Spanish colonies’ silver economy. Potosi was a literal mountain of silver, which allowed the Spanish to get rich off of the insatiable need for silver of the Qing.
Mt. Potosi was the source of silver, and Huancavelica was the mercury hub that purified the silver. They heavily used forced labor/slave labor, and the toxic mercury was extremely dangerous leading to continuous importation of slaves.

period-2/prince-henry-the-navigator.html.title: Prince Henry the Navigator
period-2/prince-henry-the-navigator.html.description:
Did not actually explore, but instead was a state sponsor for explorers for Portugal, such as Vasco Da Gama's voyages for the Portuguese. He contributed capital and economic incentive for explorers, which was critical for developing motivation to explore and find new trade routes.
This directly led to the development of new colonies and maritime empires, and Prince Henry and other state sponsors were vital to provide incentive to explorers to discover paths to new colonies.

period-2/pueblo-revolts.html.title: Pueblo Revolts
period-2/pueblo-revolts.html.description:
A revolution by the indigenous population in the Spanish colonies in New Mexico, who actually successfully drove out the Spanish.
It was caused due to forced religious assimilation, and aimed to regain their religious freedoms.
Ultimately, the Spanish returned when the Pueblo people started to run out of food, but the threat of revolt caused them to ease some of the religious restrictions they had previously imposed.

period-2/qing-empire.html.title: Qing Empire
period-2/qing-empire.html.description:
The Qing empire during this time period was still undeniably the single most influential empire in the world, evidenced by the entire world economy being propped up by the silver trade.
However, it's reliance on silver would eventually spell its doom - it would solely import silver, which is not a valuable good while exporting actually valuable luxury goods.
Still, it accumulated immense wealth, and also heavily utilized gunpowder to great effect in small scale conflicts. However, it did not develop to as great an extent of other empires and colonize other nations, only holding onto Tibet as a "colony".

period-2/queen-ana-nzinga.html.title: Queen Ana Nzinga
period-2/queen-ana-nzinga.html.description:
Ruler of the Ndongo and Matmba nations, she would negotiate treaties with the Portuguese and avoided turning the Ndongo and Matamba into other slave nations, fighting and rebelling against the Portuguese throughout her rule.
She is a key example of the African resistance to the slave trade/European colonial expansion, and shows that not every single nation devolved into slavery in Africa in order to win the arms race against other African slave states, with some African nations resisting European influence.

period-2/renaissance.html.title: Renaissance
period-2/renaissance.html.description:
Due to the influence of Dar al-Islam in P1, bringing in influence through Al-Andalus and the Crusades, Europe was undergoing a period of intellectual flourishing that encouraged technological development. This helped Europe adapt quickly to new maritime technologies that helped navigation and helped Europe catch up the the East.
Furthermore, the environment of discovery and intellectual development led to many explorers and "discoverers", which led to more voyages by nations to help create maritime empires.

period-2/russian-empire.html.title: Russian Empire
period-2/russian-empire.html.description: Russia during this time period was weakened by internal strife and the continued influence of the Mongols, but would begin attempts at Westernization and modernization during P2 which was somewhat successful for the elites, such as the Table of Ranks. Especially under the reign of Peter the Great, who created St. Petersburg as a "Window to the West", and aimed to westernize, some reforms were attempted. However, the overall social system remained the same with serfdom being a core part of the economy of Russia. The lack of modernization and westernization for most of Russia made Russia unable to westernize and industrialize in P3.

period-2/safavid-empire.html.title: Safavid Empire
period-2/safavid-empire.html.description:
Mainly notable for the fact that they are the only Shi'a gunpowder empire in Period 2, where every other nation is Sunni.
They were extremely similar to the other Muslim empires, utilizing many of the same strategies for administration, military, and economic control. The only major difference is their Shi'ism, which caused them to continuously be in conflict with the other Sunni nations around them and also led to religious intolerance.

period-2/sociedad-de-castas.html.title: Sociedad de Castas
period-2/sociedad-de-castas.html.description:
The creation of new social structures in order to justify the Spanish use of the indigenous population, it was promoted heavily by the Spanish settlers.
It was extremely detailed and government structured, with more and more indigenous blood being classified as lower class, and provided a racist and social justification for abuse of workers.
As the colonies became more diverse, especially with African slaves, the social structure became increasingly more complex in order to continue supporting the whites/Spanish at the top of the social hierarchy.
This system was created mainly in part due to the outnumbered Spanish: at 20% of the population, they were outnumbered and needed this clear social structure to limit the power of other groups and to prevent potential uprisings/rebellions if they gained too much power.**

period-2/spain.html.title: Spain
period-2/spain.html.description:
The most powerful maritime empire in period 2, but significantly declined afterwards. They had most of their colonial holdings in the Americas, notably the Caribbean and Latin America.
They would produce many cash crops such as sugar, coffee, cacao, but none of these were really as important as silver. Spain is important for introducing one of the biggest sources of American silver, and by trading it to China, would finally create a global trading network.
However, they over relied on silver, and when inflation hit, their wealth would suddenly collapse especially due to a lack of diversification and economic development due to multiple wars.

period-2/stono-rebellion.html.title: Stono Rebellion
period-2/stono-rebellion.html.description:
The largest slave rebellion in the British colonies, in South Carolina slaves aimed to collect arms and march to Florida where the Spanish offered them freedom.
Ultimately, the rebellion was put down and led to harsher restrictions, but is still an example of attempted resistance against the British empire.

period-2/table-of-ranks.html.title: Table of Ranks
period-2/table-of-ranks.html.description:
The Table of Ranks was a meritocratic system in which service to the Tsar and ability/skills were theoretically the only way in which to climb the ranks. This meant that money/influence in the empire couldn’t get unskilled family members into powerful positions. However, it was slowly undermined later on, as Tsars made the requirements more lenient.
This helped the Tsar maintain their power easily as noble uprisings were more difficult as the Tsar maintained direct control of the social/political structure. Thus, this also allowed the Tsar to gain a highly skilled administrative structure.
This allowed the Russian Empire to have a lot of skilled governors/leaders/bureaucrats to administrate throughout the empire, and it also centralized power, as aristocrats/noble families couldn’t leverage their power as much if it was directly granted from the Tsar.

period-2/taj-mahal.html.title: Taj Mahal
period-2/taj-mahal.html.description:
Taj Mahal was a huge mausoleum, being built at huge cost at the height of the Mughal empire under Shah Jahan.
The immense Taj Mahal legitimized the Mughal empire by demonstrating their immense wealth and prosperity to their population, showing how prosperous the empire was.
It also demonstrates religious syncretism, as it merges both Persian (read: Muslim) and Indian elements of architecture.

period-2/tax-farming.html.title: Tax Farming
period-2/tax-farming.html.description:
The Iltizam, tax farming under the Ottomans, where the rights to taxing a certain piece of land were auctioned off and sold to the highest bidder. The Iltizam allowed the Ottomans to gain money to fund their extensive military campaigns, but eventually the ottomans would have to cut back and find other ways to raise cash, as slowly they lost more and more revenue Similar to Zamindars, sometimes sacrifices had to be made for effective taxation and economic growth.The Safavids also had similar methods of tax farming. Similar to the Ottomans and Mughals, they would grant local leaders the right to tax in exchange for quick cash.
See Mansabdars and Zamindars.

period-2/tokugawa-shogunate.html.title: Tokugawa Shogunate
period-2/tokugawa-shogunate.html.description:
The Sakoku edict of 1635 was an example of resistance to European imperialist expansion, as Japan closes themselves off to foreigners completely (except for the less-religious Dutch).
This edict is an example of resistance because it shows Asian countries preventing Europeans from enroaching onto their territory, and demonstrates how European powers are not powerful enough to force the Eastern nations directly.

period-2/transatlantic-trade.html.title: Transatlantic Trade

period-2/yasak-system.html.title: Yasak System
period-2/yasak-system.html.description: The Yasak collection was a tax collection system in Russia, of tribute that amounted to basically fur taxation. This taxation policy was created because of the fur trade from Russia to Europe - without the fur tribute, Russia couldn’t have enough fur to trade with Western Europe for goods that they needed. By constructing a stable taxation system, similar to the Qing single whip system, it kept the economy more stable and helped create a "unified currency" for taxation, helping the Russian Empire maintain stronger economic control over their empire.

period-2/zamindars.html.title: Zamindars
period-2/zamindars.html.description:
Extremely similar to the Mansabdars, but they were hereditary and only served as intermediaries between the people and the ruler. Similar to the Mansabdars, they became corrupt and splintered the Mughal empire. Essentially they served the same role as the Mansabdars in terms of tax collection, allowing the empire to be able to collect taxes over their huge territory.
Thus, they show that the Mughals had to sacrifice some amount of centralized control for effective taxation policies, similar to the Yasak System where furs were taken as tax but natives had different methods of tax collection.

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site

bodyClasses: publish css-settings-manager mod-windows styled-scrollbars show-inline-title show-ribbon is-focused
